abstract: This article investigates the origins of the traditional Trinitarian formula to determine the extent to which the Nicene definition was shaped by Greek philosophy. By engaging with contemporary theological debates, the research compares the traditional meta-physical formulation of the Trinity with the biblical revelation of the Godhead. The study concludes that the Trinitarian formula should perhaps be viewed less as a rigid dogmatic system and more as a "dehellenized" mystery. It argues that the Church Fathers and the post-Nicene church adopted Greek philosophical and theological terminology—concepts often already latent in the doxological and liturgical structures of Scripture—primarily as a tool for contextualization rather than as a departure from the biblical worldview.
